---
title: "Forecasting HDB Resale Prices with Ensemble Learning"
subtitle: "Applied Machine Learning"
author: "Wu Jiayan and project team"
date: "2026-04-05"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: true
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
---

## Project overview

This project develops a machine-learning estimator for future Singapore HDB resale prices and tests whether ensemble learning can outperform standalone models under genuine future-market conditions.

::: {.callout-note}
## Research question
Can a stacking ensemble reduce forecasting error on future transactions when compared with linear, tree-based, and boosting baselines?
:::

## Data and validation design

The analysis uses a chronological split to avoid temporal leakage.

| Partition | Transaction period | Rows | Purpose |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Training | Up to 2023 | 169,150 | Model fitting and time-series cross-validation |
| Validation | 2024 | 27,832 | Model selection |
| Test | 2025 onward | 27,683 | Locked future evaluation |

Hyperparameters were tuned with five-fold time-series cross-validation. Each validation fold occurs after its training fold, matching the intended forecasting use case more closely than a random split.

## Feature engineering

The modelling dataset combines:

- Property attributes: floor area, flat type, flat model, storey midpoint, and remaining lease.
- Location attributes: town, coordinates, MRT distances, and nearest-school distance.
- Time attributes: transaction year and continuous time index.
- Structural-event indicators: post-COVID period, December 2021 cooling measures, September 2022 cooling measures, and higher-interest-rate period.
- One-hot encoded categorical variables.

The target is resale price per square metre. A log transformation is applied through `TransformedTargetRegressor`.

## Models

The comparison includes Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and a stacking ensemble. The ensemble combines tuned Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Ridge base estimators with a Ridge meta-model.

## Results

| Model | Test MAE | Test RMSE | Test R² |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Linear Regression | 849.92 | 1,020.71 | 0.6261 |
| Ridge | 849.83 | 1,020.56 | 0.6263 |
| Lasso | 839.79 | 1,013.60 | 0.6313 |
| Decision Tree | 836.01 | 1,025.20 | 0.6229 |
| Random Forest | 827.34 | 985.85 | 0.6513 |
| Gradient Boosting | 803.08 | 935.77 | 0.6858 |
| **Stacking Ensemble** | **725.87** | **865.13** | **0.7314** |

Relative to Ridge, the stacking ensemble reduces RMSE by approximately 155 per square metre, reduces MAE by approximately 124 per square metre, and improves R² by about 10 percentage points.

## Conclusion

The stacking ensemble provides the strongest future-period performance. The result supports using complementary nonlinear learners when modelling a housing market affected by location, property characteristics, policy interventions, and changing macroeconomic conditions.

## Limitations and next steps

- Monitor feature and price drift after deployment.
- Retrain periodically using newly completed transactions.
- Confirm that every feature is available at prediction time.
- Add prediction intervals and subgroup error analysis by town and flat type.
- Compare the ensemble with modern gradient-boosting libraries under the same temporal protocol.

## Source artifacts

- [Research report](Research%20Report.docx)
- [Ensemble methods notebook](RQ4%20Ensemble%20Methods.ipynb)
